title: Log Analytics & KQL basics
summary: Log Analytics is the massive database for unstructured events. Kusto Query Language (KQL) is the incredibly fast, pipe-based language you use to interrogate it.
analogy: Log Analytics is a massive, endlessly growing warehouse of filing cabinets. KQL is the hyper-efficient librarian who can instantly fetch exactly the files you need by passing them through a series of filters.
diagram:
flowchart LR
  Source[App Logs / Security Events] -->|Ingest| LAW[(Log Analytics Workspace)]
  LAW -->|KQL Query| Filter1[where Level == 'Error']
  Filter1 -->|Pipe| Summarize[summarize count by App]
  Summarize -->|Result| Chart[Render Chart]
body:
## First principles

When investigating an incident, you need to search billions of lines of text (logs) instantly. Traditional relational databases (SQL) are too rigid for unstructured, schema-on-read log data. You need a dedicated big-data indexing engine and a language optimized for filtering streams of text.

## Why it exists

Before Log Analytics, engineers `grep`ped text files on individual servers. **Log Analytics Workspaces** provide a bottomless, centralized sink for logs. **Kusto Query Language (KQL)** was invented by Microsoft to query this data at breathtaking speed. It is built around a "pipeline" mental model, making it much easier to write than deeply nested SQL.

## How it works

Data is stored in **Tables** (e.g., `AppRequests`, `SecurityEvent`) inside a **Workspace**.

KQL reads top-to-bottom. You start with a massive dataset (a table) and pass it through the pipe operator `|` to filter, transform, and summarize it, whittling it down step by step.

## In practice

Here is a tiny, concrete KQL walkthrough answering the question: *"Which endpoints are throwing the most 500 errors in the last day?"*

```kusto
AppRequests                        // 1. Start with the table (all requests)
| where TimeGenerated > ago(1d)    // 2. Filter down to the last 24 hours
| where ResultCode == "500"        // 3. Filter down to only server errors
| summarize ErrorCount = count() by Url  // 4. Group by URL and count them
| order by ErrorCount desc         // 5. Sort highest first
| take 10                          // 6. Show me just the top 10
```
Notice how the data flows downward. Each `|` takes the output of the line above it.

## Gotchas

- **Time filters matter.** Always include a `where TimeGenerated > ago(...)` early in your query. If you don't, KQL will try to scan months of data, which is slow and expensive.
- **Workspaces are a security boundary.** You usually have one central Log Analytics Workspace for a team or environment, so you can query across all resources at once. Splitting logs into 50 different workspaces makes cross-resource queries incredibly painful.
teachItBack: Log Analytics Workspaces store massive amounts of telemetry data. We query that data using KQL, which uses a pipe structure to filter and shape data step-by-step. You always start with a table, filter by time, filter by condition, and then aggregate.

glossary.definition: The Azure resource that acts as a logical container for your logs and metrics data.
A read-only query language optimized for processing massive amounts of log data using a pipeline syntax.
